finding: §10.2's defining constraint is that the audit prior-round section is 'scoped to the role receiving the prompt ... not other roles' findings that this auditor cannot re-check'. Acceptance test #11 only asserts the section 'lists the previous round's non-PASS rows with role, item id, status, location, and whether the file changed since' — it requires each row to CARRY a `role` field but never asserts that rows belonging to OTHER roles are EXCLUDED from role R's prompt. An implementation that lists every role's non-PASS rows (each annotated with its `role`) satisfies test #11 verbatim while violating §10.2's core scoping rule, so two engineers would write contradictory tests for the exact behavior that is the whole point of the round-4 fix. The per-role exclusion is the testable essence of the change and it is not pinned.
suggestion: Extend test #11 (or add an item) to assert: given a prior round carrying non-PASS rows from roles A and B, the audit prompt emitted for role A contains A's rows and contains NO row whose role is B. That negative assertion is what makes the scoping deterministic.

finding: Round-4 stores id_scheme on 'every round tp records' = the round record (the --record/.tp-review artifact that carries recorded_at), but §10.3 names tp audit --merge as the consumer stable ids exist to protect, and §10.4 says --merge globs the per-role RESULT files (audit-r<N>-<role>.ndjson, written by the roles, not by --record) which carry no id_scheme marker. In the exact upgrade-mid-loop case §10.9 exists for (round-1 result file from v0.29 = positional ids, round-2 from v0.30 = slug ids), --merge's (role, item_id) dedup has no marker and must fall back to id shape, but the shapes are NOT guaranteed distinct: a subject can slug to a bare number, and §10.3's slug collision suffixes -2/-3 are indistinguishable from positional ordinals, so file-go-safety-2 (positional) can false-match file-go-safety-2 (slug). The fix is correct for the prior-round section (which reads the round record) but does not deliver the robustness §10.3 requires for the merge path. §15 item 18 tests only the prior-round section, not cross-version merge.
suggestion: Stamp id_scheme onto the result files too (emit it in the prompt so the role echoes it, or have --record/merge carry it per-file) so --merge can dedup within a scheme and refuse/flag cross-scheme matches; or state explicitly that merging a legacy (positional) result file with a slug result file is rejected with an exit code + hint and that cross-scheme (role, item_id) dedup is never attempted.

finding: §10.9 makes the `id_scheme` marker the linchpin of legacy detection: 'Every round tp records under v0.30.0+ carries an `id_scheme` marker — value "slug"', and 'detection is by the stored marker, not by parsing the id'. Acceptance test #18 verifies only the ABSENT-marker (legacy) path — a round recorded without the marker is read without error and yields a positional-id prior-round section. No acceptance item asserts the POSITIVE counterpart: that a round recorded under v0.30.0+ actually WRITES the marker, or that its value is the literal "slug". Because §10.9 says detection keys on the marker (not on id parsing), a regression that omits the marker or writes a wrong value would still pass #18 (which is keyed on absence) and #12 (which exercises slug dedup via the item_ids themselves, not via the stored marker), leaving the detection mechanism — the round-4 fix's central artifact — itself unverified.
suggestion: Add an acceptance item: after recording a round under v0.30.0+ (tp review/audit --record), the recorded round file carries `id_scheme` with value `"slug"`. This pins the positive side of §10.9 so the marker is proven written, not merely proven absent-when-legacy.

finding: The round-4 per-role scoping (‘scoped to the role receiving the prompt … that role's own prior non-PASS rows’) made the section carry only the receiving auditor's rows, yet each row still emits `role` as a field — the identical string in every row, already known to the auditor (it is their own role), and already encoded verbatim in the item id `file-<role>-<slug>` (§10.3). No auditor decision consumes this byte; it is pure per-row overhead in every audit prompt from round 2 onward. §15.11 confirms `role` is verified per row, so the redundancy is contractual, not incidental.
suggestion: Drop `role` from the per-row field list in both §10.2 and §15.11; the section scope already names the role and the item id encodes it. The four remaining per-row fields (item id, status, evidence location, file-changed-since) each feed a distinct auditor decision.

finding: §10.9 says 'Every round tp records under v0.30.0+ carries an id_scheme marker — value "slug" — stating which id scheme its rows use.' But the slug change (§10.3) is specific to file-derived AUDIT checklist items. Review rounds don't use file-<role>-* item ids at all — review --merge deduplicates on (location, class), not (role, item_id) — so writing id_scheme:"slug" on a review round's ReviewRound entry is a false descriptor. Even within audit rounds, spec-derived items (table-*, list-*) keep ids unchanged by §10.3. Since ReviewRound is the shared struct for both review_rounds and audit_rounds, literal implementation of 'every round' stamps a semantically wrong marker on review rounds. The marker should be scoped to audit rounds, or the spec should document that for non-audit rounds the value is a format-version tag, not an accurate id-scheme statement.
suggestion: Change 'every round tp records' to 'every audit round tp records' in §10.9, or add a sentence clarifying that review rounds carry the marker as a format-version indicator only (their rows have no tp-generated item ids) and the slug descriptor applies solely to file-derived audit checklist items.

finding: §11.2 classifies duration_source for the implicit-claim paths (tp done, tp commit) and the explicit-claim paths (tp claim, tp next, tp next --brief) but is silent on tp done --covered-by. §6.1 explicitly carves --covered-by out as a distinct close path for commit_files ('records none, because its work lives in the covering task'), so an implementer cannot infer by analogy whether covered-by is also special-cased for duration: does it set implicit, leave duration_source unset, or exclude the task from duration accounting entirely? §15 item 16 tests done and commit but not covered-by.
suggestion: Add one sentence to §11.2 classifying --covered-by for duration_source (e.g. 'a --covered-by close sets implicit like any implicit claim' or 'excludes the task from duration accounting') plus a matching acceptance row in §15.
